Google 


This is a digital copy of a book that was preserved for generations on library shelves before it was carefully scanned by Google as part of a project 
to make the world’s books discoverable online. 


It has survived long enough for the copyright to expire and the book to enter the public domain. A public domain book is one that was never subject 
to copyright or whose legal copyright term has expired, Whether a book is in the public domain may vary country to country. Public domain books 
are our gateways to the past, representing a wealth of history, culture and knowledge that’s often difficult to discover. 

Marks, notations and other marginalia present in the original volume will appear in this file - a reminder of this book’s long journey from the 
publisher to a library and finally to you. 


Usage guidelines 


Google is proud to partner with libraries to digitize public domain materials and make them widely accessible. Public domain books belong to the 
public and we are merely their custodians. Nevertheless, this work is expensive, so in order to keep providing this resource, we have taken steps to 
prevent abuse by commercial parties, including placing technical restrictions on automated querying. 


We also ask that you: 


+ Make non-commercial use of the files We designed Google Book Search for use by individuals, and we request that you use these files for 
personal, non-commercial purposes. 


+ Refrain from automated querying Do not send automated queries of any sort to Google's system: If you are conducting research on machine 
translation, optical character recognition or other areas where access to a large amount of text is helpful, please contact us. We encourage the 
use of public domain materials for these purposes and may be able to help. 


+ Maintain attribution The Google “watermark” you see on each file is essential for informing people about this project and helping them find 
additional materials through Google Book Search. Please do not remove it. 


+ Keep it legal Whatever your use, remember that you are responsible for ensuring that what you are doing is legal. Do not assume that just 
because we believe a book is in the public domain for users in the United States, that the work is also in the public domain for users in other 
countries. Whether a book is still in copyright varies from country to country, and we can't offer guidance on whether any specific use of 
any specific book is allowed. Please do not assume that a book's appearance in Google Book Search means it can be used in any manner 
anywhere in the world. Copyright infringement liability can be quite severe. 


About Google Book Search 





Google’s mission is to organize the world's information and to make it universally accessible and useful. Google Book Search helps readers 
discover the world’s books while helping authors and publishers reach new audiences. You can search through the full text of this book on the web 
at[http: //books . google .com/|

Lord Fairfax. —n the Peerage of Scotland 1 
find this entry : 

“ Fairfax, Baron, Charles Snowdon Fairfax, 1627, 
Baron Fairfax, of Cameron; sue. his grandfather, 
‘Thomas, ninth baron, 1846. is lordship resides at 
Woodburne, in Maryland, United States.” 

Fairfax is not a Scotch name. And I can find 
no trace of any person of that fa 
in Scotch affairs. Cameron is, 











what circumstances, the barony was creat 

Qndly. When did the family cease to possess 
land or other property in Scotland, if they ever 
held any? 

Srdly. Is the present a citizen or subject 
of the United States? it so, is he known and ad- 
dressed as Lord Fairfax, or how ? 

‘4thly. Has be, or has any of his ancestors, since 
the recognition of the United States as a nation, 
ever used or applied for permission to exercise the 
fanctions of a peer of Scotland, e. g. in the elec 
tion of representative peers? 

Sthly. Ifhe be a subject of the United States, 
and have taken, expréssly or by implication, the 
oath of citizenship (which pointedly renounces 
allegiance to our sovereign), how is it that his 
name is retained on the roll of a body whose first 
duty it is to guard the throne, and whose exist- 
ence is a denial of the first proposition in the 
constitution of his country?

to notice the third of these Queries. § W.H. M. 


Tailless Cate. — A writer in the New York 
Literary World of Feb. 7, 1852, makes mention 
of a bread of cats destitute of tails, which are 
found in the Tele of Man. Perhaps some zenerous 
Manx correspondent will say whether this is a 
fact or o Jonathan. Surcer Hronaep. 


Salellar.— Can any of your readers gainsay 
that in salicellar the cellar is a mere corruption 
of saliére ? A list of compound words of Saxon 
and French origin might be curious.  -H.F.B. 


Arms and Motto granted to Col. William Carlos. 
—Can any reader of “N. & Q" give the date of 
the grant of arms to Col. William Carlos (who 
assisted Charles II. to conceal himself in the 
“ Royal Oak,” after the battle of Worcester), and 
specify the exact terms of the grant P - 


Naval Atrocitie In the article on “ Wounds,” 


in the Encye. Brit. 4th edition, published 1810, 
the author, after mentioning the necessity of a 














any man is pronounced by the surgeon to be mor- 
tally wounded, be is forthwith, while still living 

conscious, thrown overboard,” or words to 
this effect, as I quote from memory. That such 
horrid barbarity was not practised in 1810, it is 
needless to say; and if it bad been usual at any 
revious period, Smollett and other writers who 
Reve exposed with unsparing Land all the defects 
in the naval system of their day, would have 
scarcely left this unnoticed when’ they attack 
much slighter abuses. If such a thing over oc- 
curred, even in the worst of times, it must have 
been an isolated case. I have not met elsewhere 
with any allusion to this or the atrocity 
record tnd would be glad of more inform 


















ation on the subject. . 8, Wane, 
Turlehydes. — During the great famine in Ire- 
land in 1331, it is said that — 
“The people in their distress met with an unex- 


pected and providential relief For about the 24th 
June, a prodigions number of large sea fish, ealled 
turlehydes, were brought into the bay of Dublin, and 
east on shore at the mouth of the river Dodder. 
‘They were from thirty to forty feet long, and so 
bulky that two tall men placed one on each side of the 
fish could not see one another."— The Histury and 
Antiquitien of the City of Dublin from the Earliest 
‘Accounts, by Walter Harris, 1766, p. 265. 

This account is com from several records of 
the time, some of which still exist. As the term 
turlehydes is not known to Irish scholars, can any 
of the readers of “N. & Q” say what precise 
animal is, meant by it, or give any derivation oF 
reference for the term ? U.U. 


Dublin, 


Foreign Orders — Queen of Bohemia. —It is 
well known that in some foreign Orders the 
decorations thereof are conferred upon ladies. 
Can any of your correspondents inform me 
whether the Order of the Annunciation of Sar- 
dinia, formerly the Order of the Ducal House of 
Savoy, at any time conferred its decorations upon 
ladies ; and whether the Princess Elizabeth, after- 
wards Queen of Bohemia, ever had the decoration 
of any Forsign order conferred upon her? Ina 
portrait of her she is represented with o star or 
adge upon the upper part ofthe left arm, 


Pickard Family.—Is the Pickard, or Picard, 
family, a branch of which is located in Yorkshire, 
of Norman origin ? If so, who were the first settlers 
in England ; and also in what county are they most 
numerous ? Ong oF tar Fauitr. 


Bradford.

Explanation of the Word “ Miser.” —Can any 
of your readers explain how and when miser came 
to get the menning of an avaricious hoarding man ? 
In Spenser's Faerie Queene, 11. 1. ‘used in 
ite nearly primary sense of “ wretcl 
“ Vouchsafe to stay your steed for humble miser’s sake.” 
Again, Faerie Queene, 1. 3. 8.: 

«The miser throw himself, as an offal, 
Straight at his foot in base hut 

In Milton's Comus, which was written about 
fifty years after the first three books of the Faerie 
Queene, the present signification of the word is 
complete : 

~ You may as well spread out the unsuna’d heaps 

Of miser's treasure by an outlaw's den, 
And tell me it is afe, as bid one hope 
‘Danger will sink on opportunity,” &c. 
J.D. Ganprer. 











Bottisham, 
[The modern restricted use of the word miser is 
‘subsequent to Shakespeare's time; for in Part I. King 


Henry VI, Act V. Se. 4., 


« Deorepit miser / base ignoble wretch !* 
Stevens says hes no relation to avarice, but simply means 
a miserable creature. So in the interlude of Jacob and 
Boaw, 1568: 

But as for these misere within my father’s tent.” 
Again, in Lord Stirling's tragedy of Cresus, 1604: 
“Or think'st thou me of judgement too remiss, 
‘A miser that in miserie remains.” 
Otway, however, in bis Orphas, published in 1680, 
‘uses it for a covetous person : 
“ Though she be dearer to my soul than rest 
To weary pilgrims, or to misers gold, 
Rather than wrong Castalio, I'd forget thee.” 
80 also does Pope : 
“No silver saints by dying misers given, 
Here brib'd the rage of ill-requited heaven,”] 


“ Acis and Galatea.” —Is there any good evi 
dence in support of the commonly received opi 
that the words to Handel's Acis and Galatea were 
written by Gay? Hawkins merel ites that 
they “are said to have been written by Mr. Gay.” 
I have no copy of Burney at hand to refer 
but I find the same statement repeated by various 
other musical historians, without, however, any 
authority being given for it, The words in ques- 
tion are not to be found among” the Poems on 
several Occasions, by Mr. John Gay, published in 
1767 by Tonson and others. Have they ever 
been included in any collective edition ‘of 
works ? G.T. 

Reading.

compiled by Thomas Oliphant, Esq., it is stated that

ish Poets, 1790, as well as in 
Chalmers's edition of 1810, and in the complete edi« 
tion of British Poets, Edinburgh, 1794.] 


Birm-bank.—The bank of a canal opposite to 
the towing-path is called the birm-bank. What 
is the derivation of this ? Uxepa, 

Philadelphia. 

[The word birm'seems to have the same meaning as 
berme (Fr, berme), which, in Fortification, denotes a 
picce of ground of three, four, or five feet in width, 
eft between the rampart and the moat or foss, designed 
to receive the ruins of the rampart, and prevent the 
earth from filling the foss. Sometimes it is palisaded, 
and in Holland ia generally planted with quickset 
hedge.

his history ? Sunvizns.

Gage is given in the Georgion Era, vol. ii. p. 67.]

RAPPING NO NOVELTY. 
(Vol. viii. pp. 512. 632.) 

The story referred to is feelty s very curious 
one, and I'should like to know whether it is ex- 
actly as it was told by Baxter, especially as there 
seems to be reason for believing that De Foe 
(whom on other grounds one would not trust in 
such a matter) did not take it from the work 
which he quotes. Perhaps if you can find room 
for the statement, some correspondent would be 
good as to state whether it has the sanction of 

ter: 





his Historical Discourse of; 
ein now io London att bos 
derstanding, sober, pious man, oft one of my hearers, 
who bas an elder brother, a gentleman of considerable 
rank, who having formerly seemed pious, of late years 
does often fall into the sin of drunkenness; he often 
lodges long together here in his brother's house, and 
whensoever he is drunk and has slept himself sober, 
something knocks at his bed's head, as if one knocked. 
‘on a wainscot. When they remove his bed it follows 
jim, Besides other loud noises on other parts where 
is that all the house hears, they have often watched 
im, and kept his bands lest he should do it himeelf,

Catholic Bible Society (Vol. vilig p.494.).— 
Ma. Corrow will find some account of this So- 
ciety (the only one I know of) in Bishop Milner's 
Supplementary Memoirs of the Evgtish Catholics, 

ubfished in the year 1820, p. 999. It published 
8 stereotype olition of the New Testament with- 
out the usual distinction of verges, and very few 
notes. The whole schemo was soverely reprobated 
by Dr. Milner, on grounds stated by him in, the 
Appendix to the Memoirs, p. 302. “The Societ; 
soon expired, and no tracty or reports were, 
believe, ever published by it. Lhe correspondence 
between Mr. Charles Butler and Mr. ‘Bisir will 
be found in the Gentleman's Magazine for the 7

ommunications with Iceland. —In the summer

Judging by the character of the handwriting, 
L would say thet the above-mentioned lines weru 
written not later than the thne of Edward I; and 
as many of the vellum leaves of this book have 
been sally disfigured, as well by the pressure of 
lips as by tincture of galls, I am inclined so think 
that official oaths were formerly taken in tha 
Court of Exchequer of Jivland by presenting the 
book when opened to ths person about to. be 
sworn in the manner at this day used (aa we are 
informed by Honoré de Mareville) in the Eccles 
siastical Court at Guernsey. 
Tt appears by an entry in one of the Order 
Books of the Exchequer, deposited in the Excher 
juer Record Office, Four Courts, Dublin, that in 
james J."s time the oath of allegiance was taken 
upon bended knee. The entry to which T refer is 
in the following words : 
“ Raster Term, Wednesday, 20nd April, 1618.— 
is ‘of the court, the 
ice thrensurer, andl alt the barons being 
present on the bench, the lord chauncellor came hither 
‘and presented before them Thomas Hibbotts, esq. with, 
his Majesty's letters patents of the office of ehaunesilor 
of this court to him graunted, to hold and execute the 
sold offic during his naturail life which being read 
the said lord ehauncellor first ministred unto him the 
oath of the King’s supremacy, which hee tooke kncel~ 
ing on his knec, and presently after ministred unto 
im the oath ondayned for the said officer, as the same 
in coutayned of record in the redd booke of this court; 
all which being donn the mid lord chaunecllor placed 
his on the beneh on the right hand of the lord threx- 
surer, and thea departed this eourt.”

LONGPELLOW'S OMIGINALITY. 
(Vol. viii, p. 583.) 

J.C. B. has noted the similayty of thought, 
tnd even sometimes of expression,” between “The 
Reaper and the Flowers| of this popular writer, 
and a cong by Luise Reichardt, But » far more 
extraordinary siilarity than, this exists between 
‘Mr, Longfollow’s translation of @ certain Anglo- 
Saxon metrical fragment, entitled “The Grave ™ 
(Hogg’s edit, in London Domestic Library, p- 283.)

Highnew gaa ihe word of commend to his segment, 
and wore in bis military helmet ‘on ovk bough. ‘The 
Prince of Wales gave an entertainment afterwards to 
the officers at the Bush Inn, at Staines.”

Abbott Families.—Samuel Abbott, of Sudbury, 
in the county of Suffili, gentleman, lived about 
1670. Can any of your genealogical’ contributors 
inform me if he was in any way connected with the 
family of Archbishop Abbott or otherwise: lucie 
date his parentage? Tt may probably be interestin 
to persons of the same name to he acquainted th 
the pears worn by many of the Abbot family ara 
merely a corruption of the ancient inkhorns of 
the Abbots of Northamptonshire, and impaled in 
Netherheyford churchyard, same county, on the 
tomb of Sir Walt, Mauntele, Knight, and is wife 
Elizabeth, daughter of John Abbot, Esq., 1487, 
viz. a chev. between three inkhorns.’ ‘The resem- 
blance between pears and inkhorns, doubtless 
occasioned the error. I believe the ancient bottles 
of Harebottle were similarly corrupted into icicles, 

J.T. Aunore.

* Good wine needs no bush (Vol. viii, p.607.). 
—The custom of hanging out bushes of ivy, 
‘boughs of trees, or bunches of flowers, at private 
houses, as a sign that good cheer may be had 
within, still prevail in the city of Gloucester at 
the fair held at Michaelmas, called Barton Fair, 
from the locality ; sn at the three mops,” ot 
hiring fuira, on the three Mondays followinyy, to 
indicate that ule, beer, cider, &. are there sold, 
on the strength @ belitve) of an ancient privilege 
enjoyed by the inhabitants of that street to Pa 
liquors, without the usual license, during the fair.

‘Tite ASTEROIDS, HTC. 
(Vol. ix., p. 38.) 


have been scattered either by internal convulsion 
or external violence. By looking into the con= 
stitution und powers contained within our own 
earth, we koow that the means are not. wanti 
to rend us asunder under the combined effects 
volesnic action, intense heat, and water, meeting 
deep within the substance of tbe earth ander great 
pressure. 

Tlowever, there is much to be said against the 
theory of Olbers, ada eases its plausibility. 
‘The distance between the internal asteroid Floray 
and the external one Hygeia, exceeds ninety mil~ 
lions of miles; or nearly the distance between the 
carth and the sun The force which could shatter 
a world into frngments, and drive them asunder 
to such an extent, must indeed be tremendous. 

‘Mr, Hind bas drawn attention to the singular 
fact, that the asteroids “appear to separate the 
planets of small mass from the greater bodica of 
the system, the planets which rotate on their axes 
in about the same time as our earth from those 
which are whirled round in lees half that 
time, though of ten times the diametor of the 
earth; and,” he continues, “it may yet be found 
that these small bodies, ao far from being portions

‘Lio, 225, 





(from the verb), state of pevishingy frees cold or 
anger," isto be found, and thus defined, in An 
Appendix to Dr. Jolinson's English Dictionary,” 
published along with the Tntter, by William Maver, 
in 2 vols, 8v0,, Glnagow, 1809, how forty-five years 
ago. In his profane to this Appendix he says 
“Tn the compilation the editor 
debted to Mr, Mayon, whose: 
deficiencies of De, Johnson's Dictionary have eo much 
‘enricbed the voosbulary of our Iangdage, that every 
purteliter of the quarto edition should avail himself of 
4 copy of Mr. Mason's Supplement.” 


Whether or not Mr, Maver drew the word 
“starvation” from Mr. Mason's Supplement, I 
cannot say; but from old date in the west’ of 
Scotland {thas been, and is still, popularly and 
extensively used in the exact senses given to it by 
‘Mr. Maver as above. I think it much more likels 
to be of Scottish than of American origin, and 
that Mr. Webster may have picked it up from 
some of our natives in this country. 

T may adi, that in early life T often spoke with 
Mr. Maver, who was a most intelligent literary 
man. In 1809 he followed the business of a book~ 

i but from somo canse was not 


end such as have beo 
ince his time,” with * the pronanoi 
ing to the present practice of the beat erat 
orthoopiats” of the whole language. 


This word was first introduced into the English 
Tanguage by Mr. Dundas, in a dcbate in the House 
of Commons on American airs, in 1775. From 
it he obtained the nick-name of “Starvation 
Dundas” (Vide the Correspondence betwooen Ho~ 
race Walpole oud Masom vol.ii. pp- 177. 310. 996. 
edition 1851.) The word i of irzegular formation, 
the root starve being OL English, while the tex~ 
ination «ation is Latin. E.G.R.

compiled his Dictionary =

The true origin and moaning of fortorn 
has no doubt been fully explained in “ N.& 
Vol. viliy p. 569. lson's Dictionary dows 
not countenance this view, but his examplo proves 
it conclusively. THe only Mires ‘one quotation, 
from North's Plutarch; and as it stands in the 
dictionary, it isnot easy to comprehend the pns~ 
sage entirely. On comparing it, however, with 
the corresponding passage in Langhorne (Val 
edition, vol. ii, p, D7.), and again with Pompei's 
Ttatlan’ version’ (voli. p. 49.9 T havo no deube 
that, by the term forlarn hope, North implied 
merely un advanced party ; for as he is describing 
a pitched batile and not a siege, a modern forlorn

Archepricat in the Diocese of Exeter (Vol. xy 
p-106.).——Haccombe is doubtless the parish, it 
‘the diocese of Exeter, where ‘wasnn will 
find the arch-psiest. about whom he fa. inquiring. 
Hascombe fa s emaall puri, having two Bouses fa 
it, the munor-house of the Carew ferily and 
parsonage, It is said dnt by a grant from the 
Erowny in consequence of services done by an. ane 
cestor of the Carews, this parish received certain 
Privileges and exemptions one of which was that 
the priest of Hnocombe should be exempt from 
ait Ordinary spietal facaietin. “Hence the 
title of arch-priest, and that of chorepiscopus, 
which the priests of Flacooaube have claisuedy a 
perlinps sometimes received, ‘The incumbent of 
Bibury, in Gloucestershire, used to claim similar 
titles, and like exemption from spiritual juris 
diction, J. Bansose, 

















Since sending my Query on this subject, I have 
obtained the following Informati e Rectory 
of Haccombe, whi x peculiar one, in the 
diocese of Exeter, gives to its incumbent for the 
time being the ‘dignity of arch-priest of the 
diocese. ‘The nrchepriest wears lawn sleeves, and, 
‘on all ocensions takes precedence after the bishoy 

‘The late rector, the Rey. TC, Carew, I am told, 
constantly officiated in lawn sleeves attached to 
un A.M. gown, and took the precedence duc to 
his spiritual rank as arch-priest of the diocese. 
‘The present arch-priest and Rector of Haccombe 
is the Bev. Fitzwilliam J. Taylor, Does such an 
office, or rather dignity, exist in any other caso in 
the Anglican Church ? Wa, Fuasen, BCL,

in solving some or all of the following difficultios. 

1, Where does Panormitan or Tudesehis (Come 
mentar. in Quinque Libros Decretalium) apply the 
term nullatenenses to titular and utopian bishops ?

of Onery, speaking of the monks of Bangor, term

hie to come other person, containing the expres-

fanetorum infamant animas; xi pucros in clogaium 
coracull elidunts si molta miracala sireulatoriie pre

snfeneruats quanto magia," &e.

demons seems distinctly alluded to. How like 
‘the modern system! ‘The context of this passage,

colneldence between modern and ancisnt preten~ 
sions of this class. B. HL ¢.

Black Rat (Vol. vii. p. 200,).—In reply to the 
question of Mu, Simmuzx Hraoean, whether the 
original rat of this country is still in existence, T 
may mention, chat in the agricultural districts of 
Forfurshire, the Black Rat (Mus rattus) was in 
existence a few years ago. On pulling down the 
romains of an old farm-steading in 1623, after the 
building of 8 new one, they were there so nume- 
rous, that a greyhound L had destroyed no fewer 
than seventy-seven of them in the course of a 
couple of hours, Having used precautions against 
their lodgment in the new steading, under the 
floors, and on the tops of the party walls, they 
were effectually baalehed from the form. 

Hexer Sreruens, 


Blue Belle of Scotland (Vol. viiin ps 988.).— 
Your correspondent GH, of Philadelphia isi 
error in supposing that the beautiful song, 
Bells of Scotland” has any refercace to bella 
painted blue, ‘That charming melody refers to a 
very common pretty flower in Scotland, the Came 
panula latifolia of Linnwus, the flowers of which 
tare drooping and bell-shaped, and of a blue colour, 

Hasay Sturuuxs. 


Grammars, ye. for Public Schools (Vol. ix. 
p- 8, &.).—Pray add to the Tist a Latin gram> 
mary under tho title of The Common Accilence 
Improved, by the Rev. Edward Owen, Rector of 
Warrington, and for fifly years Master of the 
Gratamar School founded in that town, under the 
will of Sic Thomas Boteler, on April 27, 1626. I 
believe it was frat published in 1770, but the copy 
now before me is of an edition printed in 18005 
and the Preface contains a promiee (L know not 
whether afterwards fulfilled) of the early publi- 
cation of the rules, versified on the plan of Busbey 
nd Ruddiman, under the ttle of Hlementa Latina 
Motrica, FM.

words of French origin that begin with ity but

number have been iinported from the neighbours

[No. 229, 





No reference to cotemporary testimony is given 
by these historians. 

Galt, in his Lives of the Players, Lond. 1881, 
does not notice the story. 

Pope was at Bath on the 4th of February, 1741, 
as appears from his letter to Warburton of that 
date; but as he mentions his intention to return 
to London, he may have been there on the 14th. 
That he was not in the pit we may be confident ; 
that he was in the boxes is unlikely. His health 
was declining in 1739. In his letter to Swift, 
quoted in Croly's edition, vol. i. p. Ixxx., he says: 

Having nothing to tell you of my poetry, I come 
to what is now my chief care, my health and ainuse- 
ment; the first is better as to headaches, worse as to 
weakness and nerves. The changes of weather affect 
me much; the mornings are my life, in the evenings I 
am not dead indeed, but sleepy and stupid enough. I 
love reading still better than conversation, but my eyes 
fail, and the hours when most people indulge in com- 
pony, I am tired, and find the labour of the past day 
Sufficient (o weigh me down ; 10 [ hide myself in bed, 
as a bird in the nest, much ubout the same time, and rise 
and chirp in the morning.” 

T hope I have said enough to stop the farther 
growth of this story ; but before laying down my 

‘attention to the ce of

INEDITED LETTER OF LORD NELSON. 


I have in my possession a long letter written by 
Lord Nelson, sixteen days before the battle of 
‘Trafalgar, to the Right Hon. Lord Barham, who 
was at that time First Lord of the Admiralty. 
As an autograph collector, I prize it much ; and 
I think that the readers of “N. & Q.” might be 
glad to see it, It has not yet, as far as I am 
aware, been published : 





Vietory, Oct. 5th, 1805, 
‘My Dear Lord, 

On Monday the French asst Upaniah ships took 
their troops on board which had been landed on 
their arrival, and it ‘id that they mean to sail 
the first fresh Levant wind. And as the Cartha- 
gena ships are ready, and, when seen a few days 
ago, had their topsail yards hoisted up, this 
Ike a junction. - The position I have taken for 
this month, is from sixteen to eighteen leagues 
west of Cadiz ; for, although it is most desirable 
that the fleet should be well up in the easterly 
winds, yet I must guard ‘against being caught 
with a westerly wind near Cadiz: for a fleet of 
ships, with so many three-deckers, would inevit- 
ably be forced into the Straits, and then Cadiz 
would be perfectly free for them to come out with 
a westerly wind—as they served Lord Keith in 
the late war. Iam most anxious for the arrival 
of frigates: Jess than eight, with the brigs, &c., a8 
we settled, I find are absolutely inadequate for 
this service and to be with the fleet; and Spartel, 
Cape Cantin, or Blanco, and the Salvages, must 
be watched ‘by fast-sailing vessels, in case any 


squadron sho - " 
T have been obliged to send six sail of the line 
ores, &e. at Tetuan and Gi- 























to water and get a 
braltar ; for if I did not begin, I should very 





18. 1854.]

ble.” —Mr. C. Maxsrrexp Ixozenr has 
ttention to a growing corruption in the 
he word “eliminate,” and I trust he may 
‘ocheck its progress. The word garble has 
h very similar usnge, but the corrupt 
is now the only one in which it is ever 
1 it would be hopeless to try and restore 
original sense. 

riginal sense of “to garble” was a good 
a bad one; it meant a selection of the 


da discarding of the bad parts of any- 








its present meaning is exactly the reverse 
ia. By the statute T Rich. TIL ©. 11, it 

provided that no bow-staves shall be sold “ un- 
garbled :” that is (as Sir E. Coke explains it), 
until the good and sufficient be severed from the 
bad and insufficient. By statute 1 Jac. I. o. 19., 
a penalty is imposed on the sale of spices and 
drugs not “garbled;” and an officer called the 
garbler of spices is authorised to enter shops, and 
view the s and drugs, “and to garble and 
make clean the same.” Coke derives the word 
either from the French garber, to make fine, neat, 
dl ; or from cribler, and that from cribrare, to 
silt, &e. (4 Inst. 264.) 

Tt is easy to see how the corruption of this word 
has taken place; but it is not the less curious to 

are the opposite meanings given to it at 
diferent times. E8. 7. T. 


Deaths in the Society of Friends, 1852-3.—In 
“N. & Q,” Vol. vili, p. 488., appeared a com- 
at longevity of persons at 
Cleveland in Yorkshire. I send you for com- 
parison a statement of the deaths in the Society 
of Friends in Great Britain and Ireland, from the 
year 1852 to 1853, the accuracy of which may be 
Uepended on; from which it appears that one in 
three have attained from 70 to 100 years, the 
average being about 74}; and that thirty-seven 
attain from 80 to 90, and eight from 90 to 100. 
It would be useful to’ ascertain to what the lon- 
gevity of the inhabitants of Cleveland may be 
attributed, whether to the situation where they 
reside, or to their social habits. 

‘The total number of the Society was computed 
to be from 19,000 to 20,000, showing the deaths 
to be rather more than 1j per cent. per annum, 
Great numbers are total abstainers from strong 
drink.

Duchess of Mazarin's Monument,—I read yes- 
derday, in_an interesting French work, that the 
‘Vesutiful Hortense Mancini, a niece of Mazarin, 
and sister to Mary Mancini, the early love of 
Ioais XIV., after various Reregrinations, died at 
Chelsea, in England, on Ju iy 21699. Although 
‘tot an important question, I think I may venture 
to ask whether any monument or memorial of 
remarkable beauty exists at Chelsea, or in its 
neighbourhood P W. Ronson. 

{Neither Faulkner nor Lysons notices any monu- 
westal memorial to the Duchess of Mazarin, whose 
fasnces after the death of Charles II. (who allowed 
tera pension of 4,000/. per annum) were very slender, 
tomuch a0 that, according to Lysons, it was usual for 
ie nobility and others, who dined at her house, to 
Jere money under the plates to pay for their enter- 
‘uisment. She appears to have been in arrcar for the 
‘rich rates during the whole time of her residence at 
Crsee. J

‘imenbation ; hence the adjective figuratively signifies

‘i tranquil time, as it is observed about the coast of 

Sicily, from whence the proverb is transported, the 
days.”]

Imay refer Mz. B.H. Azrorp to the Oxford

fm, unless we are to consider the lions and dogs be- 
Neub their feet as emblematical of the virtues of 
range, generosity, and fidelity, indispensable to their 
Mulion: One or two dogs are often at the feet of 
tie lady, ‘They are probably intended for some fa-

Neither dog nor lion occurs at the feet of the 
flowing knights represented on brasses prior to

These examples are described or engraved in

it) would supply other examples under both of

It is usually asserted that the dog appears at 
the feet of the lady in monumental brasses as a 
symbol of fidelity ; while the lion accompanies her 
lord as the emblem of strength and courage. 
‘These distinctions, however, do not appear to have 
been much sttended to. ihe dog, in Boe saeee 
a und, very frequently a at the feet 
of a knight or Gillan, aa on the brasees of the 
Earl of Warwick, 1401, Sir John Falstolf at 
Oulton, 1445, Sir John Leventhorpe at Saw. 
bridgeworth, 1433, Sir rinald de Cobham at 
Lingfield, 1403, Richard Purdaunce, Mayor of 
Norwich, 1436, and Peter Halle, Esquire, at 
bite ae eel Sir Site Paes at a 
ride's, Glumorganshire, 1285, has dragon; an 

‘on the brass of Alan Fleming, at Newark, 1361, 
appears a lion with a human face seizing a ler 
lion. On avery late brass of Sir Edward Warner, 
at Little Plumstead, Norfolk, 1565, appears a 

hound; a full century after the date assigned 
EF 'B.H. Auronp for the cessation of these aym- 
bolical figures. 

Sometimes the lady has two little dogs, as Lady 
Bagot, at Baginton, Warwickshire, 1407; and in 
one instance, that of Lady Poryent at Digewell, 
Herts, 1415, there is a hedgehog, e meaning of 
which is sufficiently obvious. B,H.Arronp, in 
noticing the omission of the dog in the brass of 
Lady Camoys, at Trotton, 1424, has not men- 
tioned a singular substitute which is found for it, 
namely, the figure of a boy or young man, stand- 
ing by the lady's right foot: but what this means 
T cannot attempt to determine; perhaps her only 


son. 

It may be interesting to add that some brasses 
of ecclesiastics exhibit strange figures, not easy to 
interpret, if meant as symbolical, The brass at

PHOTOGRAPIIC CORRESPONDENCE. 


A Prize for the best Collodion. — Your * Hint to the 
Photographie Society * ( Feb. 25) I much approve of, 
but [have always found more promptness from indi- 
viduals than from associated bodies; and all phoio- 
graphers I deem to be under great obligations to you 
in affording us a medinm of communication before 
Photographic Society was in existence. During the 
past month your valuable articles, from some of our 
most esteemed photographists, show that your pages 
fare the agreeable medium of publivhing their re- 
searches. I would therefore reypectfully suggest that 
you should yourself offer a prize for the best mode of 
making a good useful collodion, and that that prize 
should be a complete set of your valuable jour 
which now, I belivve, is progresing with its ninth 
volume. dependent names 

it testing the merits of any sample 
supplied to you, and a condition should be that the 
la should be published in N.& Q" Your ob- 
upon the manufacturers of paper, respecting 
the intrinsic value of a premium, are equally applicable 
to this proposition, because, should the eollodion pre- | 
pared by any of the various dealers who at present ad- 
vertise in your columns be deemed to be the most satis- 
factory, your sanction and that of your friends alone 
would be an ample recompense. I would also suggest 
that samples sent to you should be labelled with 
motto, and a corresponding motto, seuled, should con 
tain the name and address, the name and address of the 
successful sample alone to be opened : this would effec- 
tually preelude all preevnecived notions entertained by 
the testing manipulators who are to decide on the 
merits of what is submitted to them. 

‘A Raapen or “N. & Q” asp a Puorocnarian, 

[We are obliged to our correspondent not only for 
the compliment he has paid to our services to photo- 
graphy, but also for his suggestion, ‘There are many 
Featons, and some sufficiently obvious, why we should 
not undertake the task proposed ; and there are as ob- 
vious reasons why it should be undertaken by the 
Photographic Society. ‘That body has not only the 
means of sceuring the best judges of such matters, 
but an invitation from such @ body would probably 
call into the fictd of competition all the best photo- 
graphers, whether professional or amateur. ]

T excite my paper proportions of satu. 
rated solution of gallic acid and aceto-nitrate of silver, 
one or two drops of each to the drachm of distilled 
water. I always plunge the bottle of gallie asid so!u- 
tion into hot water when first made, which enables it 
to take up more of the acid; on cooling, the excem 
erystallises at the bottom. This emures an eves 
strength of solution: it will keep any length of tima, 
if a snall pivee of camphor be allowed to float in it,

‘The resultant iodide from fifteen grains of nitrate 
of silver, precipitated by means of the iodide of potas 
sium, will give the requisite quantity of iodide for 
every ounce of water; or about twenty-seven graint 
of the dried iodide will produce the same effect. It 
is however far preferable, and more economical, te 
convert all waste into chloride of silver, from which 
the pure metal may be again so readily obtained.

Albumenized Paper. —T have by careful observatioa 
found that the cause of the albumen settling and dy 
ing in waving lines and blotches on my paper, ares 
from some parts of the paper being more absorbest 
than others, the gelatinous-like nature of the albavet 
assisting to retard its ready ingress into the unequal 
parts, and, consequently, that those places becoming 
the frst dried, prevented the albumen, still slovly 
Gripping over the now more wetted parts, from ruanisg 
down equally and smoothly, thereby causing a check 
to ite progress; and as at last these became sho Un 
thicker and irregular patches of albumen were de 
posited, forming the mischief in question, 

The discovery of the cause suggested to me ti 
propriety of either giving cach sheet a prolonged fot 
ing of from ten to fifteen minutes on the salted albu. 
‘men, or until every part had become fully and equally 
saturated; of, as a preliminary to the @osting 
ng up by one eorner on a line, of putting ore 
night between each sheet a damped piece of bibaloa 

ing the whole between two 
¢ other non-absorbent material. 


very likely to lead to disappuist- 


























men at work at once,

Though not exactly the recipe for saw-dust bis- 
eats which I have heard of, there is an account of 
ess of making bread from bark in Laing’s 
“Norway” (Longman's Traveller's Lib.), part 
2.20, where, om the subject of pine-tres, iti 


‘Many were standing with all their branches dead, 
‘tripped of the bark to make bread, and blanched by 
the weather, resembling white marble,—mere ghosts 
sttrees, ‘The bread is made of the inner rind next the 
‘ood, taken off in flakes like a sheet of foolseap paper, 
‘ad in steeped or washed in warm water, to clear off its 
‘ringent principle. It is then hung across a rope to 
‘4y in the sun, and looks exactly like sheets of parch- 
‘Rent, When dry it is pounded into small pieces mixed 
Yih corn, and ground into meal on the hand-mill or 
quem, It is much more generally used than I sup- 
Peed, There are districts in which the forests suffered 
‘ey considerable damage in the years 1812 and 1814, 

bad crops and the war, ihen raging, reduced 
‘Ray to bark bread. The Fjelde bonder use it, more 
lew, every year.

Brydone the Tourist (Vol. ix p.138.).— The 
literary world would feel obliged to J. M 
tell us the name of the writer of the crit 
says, “ Brydone never was on the summit of Etna.” 
Did ‘the scholars of Italy know more of what was 
done by Englishmen in Sicily in Brydone’s day 
than they do at present? How are the dates re~ 
conciled? Brydone would be 113 years old. 
Mr. Beckford, I think, must have been some 
thirteen or fourteen years younger. Brydone was 
always considered to be in his relations in life a 
man of probity and honour. I used to hear much 
of him from one nearly related to me, whose 

father was first cousin to Brydone’s wife. 
H.R. win F. 


Etymology of “Page” (Vol. ix p. 106.).— 
Pago Leckar’ page Fronsh tad Spunisy agi 
Provencal, is derived by Diez, Etymologisches 
Warterbuch der Romanischen Sprachen (Bonn, 
1853), p.249., from the Greek xaitior, This de- 
rivation is evidently the true one. I may take 
this opportunity of recommending the above-cited 
work to all persons who feel an interest in the 
etymology of the Romance languages, It is not 
only more scientific and learned, but more com- 
prehensive, than any other work of the kind. L. 


Longfellow (Vol. ix. p. 174.).— There was & 
family of the name of Longfellow resident in 
Brecon, South Wales, about fifty or sixty years 
ago, who were large landowners in the county ; 
and one of them (Tom Longfellow, alluded to in 
the lines below) kept the principal inn, “The 
Golden Lion,” in that town. Ilis son occupied a 
farm a few miles from Brecon, about thirty years 

0; and two of his sisters resided in the town, 
‘The family was frequently engaged in law auite 
(perhaps from the proverbially litigious disposition

‘agents in various places, and by Mr. Longfellow,

Hexar Geo. Touxins. 
Park Lodge, Weston-super-Mare,

name of Longfellow. He hi 
the town of Brecon, 
his grandfather was'a settler; though fr 
part of the country this last-named relative ori-

Ta a Inte Number of your miscellany, you 
sy general practice for congregations in 
Shurches to stand during the reading of the Lor 
Tryer, when it occurs in the order of Morning 
Lemons, In my experience, I do not remember 
%y mch custom prevalent in this part of the 
uatry; but may mention, as a curious and (as 
leas I know, or ever heard of) singular ex- 
‘leet kneeling at the routing of St. Mat. vi 

Bt Luke xi., that at Formby, a retired vil- 











lage on the Lancashire coast, my first cure, the 
people observed this usage. ‘The children in the 
schools were instructed to kneel whenever they 
read the section of these chapters which contains 
the Lord’s Prayer. And at the “Burial of the 
Dead,” as soon as the minister came to that por- 
tion of the ceremony where the use of the Lord's 
Prayer is enjoined, all the assembled mourners 
Gola and young, and however cold or damp the 
fay) would devoutly kneel down in the chapel 
yard, and remain in this posture of reverence until 
the conclusion of the service. I observed that 
their Roman Catholic neighbours, who often at- 
tended at funerals, when they happened to be 
present, did the same. So that it seemed to be 
“a tradition derived from their fathers,” and 
handed down “ from one generation to another.” 


R.L. 
Great Lever, Bolton. 


This custom is observed in the Cathedral at 
Norwich, but not (Ihelieve) in the other churches 
in that city. I remember secing it noticed in a 
very old number of the Gentleman's Magazine, 
and should be glad if any of your correspondents. 
could tell me which number it is. I have looked 
through the Index in vain. ‘The writer denounced 
it as a Popieh custom! Ww. 

















A dead Sultan, with his Shirt for an Ensign 

ix, p.76.).—Mz. Wauen will find_a long 
and interesting description of Saladin in Knolles 
Turkish History, pp. 83. 57., published in London 
by Adam Islip in 1603. I take from this learned 





work the following curious anecdote 








is not stated) 
the greatest terrour of 
ians; who, mindfull of man’s fragilitie, and 
of worldly honours, commanded at the time 
jeath no solemnitie to be vsed at his buriall, but 
only his shirt in manner of an ensigne, made fast vito 
the point of a lance, to be carried before his dead bodie 
asanensigne. A plaine priest going before and ery- 
aloud vnto the people in this sort: * Saladin Con 
querour of the East, of all the greatnesse and riches hee 
had in thia life, carrieth not with him after his death any= 
thing more than hit "—" A sight (siys Knolles) 
woorthie so great a king, as wanted nothing to his 
cternall commendation, more than the true knowledge 
of his salvation in Christ Jesu.”

“ Hord maet of laet” (Vol. ix., p. 148.).— One 
of your correspondents desires an explanation of 
this phrase, which he found in the corner of an 
old Dutch picture, It is a Flemish proverb; I 
translate it thus : 

“ Keep within bounds, though ‘tis late.” 

It may either be the motto which the artist 

adopted to identify his work while he concealed 





258 NOTES AND QUERIES. [No. 229. 
his name ; or it may be descriptive of the picture, |“ The Common-place Book” (Vol. ix., 
which then would be an illustration of this pro- | p. 174. he editor of this publication was 





yerb. Inscribed cither by the artist himself, or 
by some officious person, who thus “tacked the 
moral full in sight. 

I think I have seen a similar inscription some- 
where in Flanders on an antique drinking-cup, 
& very appropriate place for such wholesome 
counsel, 

I should like to know the subject of the picture 

correspondent refers to. In modern Dutch 
‘the proverb reads thus: 


 Houd maat of laat.” 
E. F. Woopmax. 


‘The above Dutch proverb menns, in English : 


« Keep within bounds, or leave off.”

[No. 230. 





‘Mujesty’s special mandate; as also the letters 
written by your Majesty to the Grand Master of 
the Order of Malta in favour of the above-men- 
tioned, and those from the said Grand Master in 
reply; and it is evident to me, after mature ex- 
amination, that your Majesty has done so much, 
and that it is proved that the sentence of the 
Maltese Tribunal against the said Roger Fowke 
‘was pronounced contrary to right and justice (as 
is clearly shown in the letters written by your 
Majesty to the Grand Master) ; that therefore it 
appears to be incumbent on me only to set forth 
to your Majesty, anil to the Lords of the Privy 
Council, whether it be my opinion that suflici 
satisfaction bas been given by the Grand Master's 
letters to your Majesty, who by the above-cited 
letters demand restitution ; and if not, whether in 
consequence it be lawful to your Majesty to grant 
the so-called letters of reprisal, on which subject 
I beg humbly to submit to your Majesty, and to 
the singular prudenee and judgment of the above- 
mentioned Lords, this my opinion ; that is to say, 
that the answers of the Grand Master are so far 
from being in any way satisfactory, that from them 
it may be easily perceived that the above-men- 
tioned Grand Master, although he does not deny 
‘in express terms reparation for his loss to the 
above Roger, nevertheless docs not decree any- 
thing certain on this head ; from which your Ma- 
jesty may reasonably conclude that the said re 
aration was refused. Nor does it tend to his 
lefence that he asserts that all that was done by 
his tribunal was done by solemn sentence, that 
the judges were men of great reputation, and 
that’ it is to be believed that the reasons pro- 
duced by both sides were justly considered; for 
judicial, authority is not of the same value as re- 
‘gards foreigners and subjects. It is not Inwful 
for subjects to demand a re-examination of the 
sentence pronounced by their superiors, although 
to foreign princes it entirely appertains to make 
such demand, in cases interesting themselyes or 
their subjects; other if all given sentences 
were considered as freeing nations from reprisals, 
such decrees might perhaps be obtained in any 
ease, even though manifestly unjust ; and conse- 
quently it is by all agreed to be o just cause for 
reprisals, not only when justice is not rendered, 
but also when in any case, not of a doubtful 
nature, judgment may have been given against 
right; although certainly, in cases of a doubtful 
nature, the presumption would be in favour of 
those who may have Iwen elected as public 
judges. Had the Grand Master indicated to 
Pour Majesty that the eakd Roger Fowke might 
ave preferred an appeal against the sentence 
pronounced against him to a superior tribunal, 
‘and that by the neglizence of the said Roger the 
first sentence bad become affirmed, in that case 
the remedy demanded by your Majesty would 









































have been untenable; but the ssid Grand Master 
makes no mention of such appeal : I am therefore 
of opinion that nothing in the law of nations 
could militate against the lawfulness of your Ma 
Jesty's granting letters of repriaal in the manner 


demanded. 
(Signed) Rosser Wrersux.” 


Without doubt the law of nations would war 
rant our extorting from the hands of your high 
ness’ subjects, by issuing letters of reprisal, 
which we have not been able to obtain after » 
many years by means of the letters written iz 
favour of our beloved subject and friend ; and the 
deplorable state of the said Roger requires thatwe 
should now exact by our own authority that which 
we have in vain sought to obtain by mean of 
simple communications. But taking into serious 
consideration the lamentable present state of 
Christianity, and the daily augmentation of the 
lnrge empire of our common enemy, and how dix 
tinguished has been the valour of the Maltese 
knights, always constantly exposing themselves 
a bulwark to so pertinacious an enemy, it would 
be very painful to us to be compelled to have re- 
course to reprisals, or to any such severe mode of 
proceeding, for the reparation of the loss. Tea 
glory also of the Christian name, so often 
defended, bas caused us willingly to believe that 
we must not yet despair of obtaining from your 
highness’ zathority, that reparation for bis los 
which our subject hopes to obtain by reprisal, and 
therefore, putting aside the remedy of right, and 
our Privy Bounel persuading us to milder mete 
sures, we have thought proper by this letter ® 
seriously request your highness, by that justice 
which is tho duty of princes, and of the defendem 
of Christianity, to deign to procure without delay 
to our trustworthy subject, who has suffered ® 
great an injustice from the Maltese Tribunal, sad 
who is exhausted by the delays of so many yesh 
full compensation for all his raex, including so 
the amount of his expenses ; so that we may neve 
lave cause to regret that we, putting aside te 
Jaw of nations, have till now abstained from re 
pris and so that henceforth the world may e® 
Jogise the Maltese as not being less just thet 
valiant, : 

We have only now to recommend your higk 
ness and all your Knights to the most good 
most great God. 

Given in our Palace of Whitehall on the 29 
day of April, of the year of Human Redemptioa 
1668, and of our reign the twentieth, 

Your Highness’ 
Cousin and Friend, 
Cuanzss Rex. 


Raphael Cottoner, to whom the last letter ™ 
addressed, ascended the Maltese throne in Or 
ber, 1663, on the decease of his brother Baplt

recently delivered to me your Majesty's above- | 
mentioned letter ; which I having 
to communicate to my Council, I 
the venerable brethren Henry de Estampes Va- 
laneay, the Grand Prior of Campania, and Don | 
Gregory Caraffa, Prior of Rocella, should be de- 
puted commissioners to examine this ease. And 
they having heard what the said Ansely had to, 
say, offered to him in my name, and in that of all | 
my Order, an opportunity to make an appeal 
which hal been deserted; but the said Ansely, | 
for want of proper authority as he stated, did not 
accept the proposition. i 

‘Such being the case, I reverently submit to 
your most serene Majesty the following argu- 
iments, to which I carnestly entreat your Majesty | 
to apply your Royal attention, and your Majesty's 
accustomed serenity and clemency. \ 

Tn the first place, it is possible that the said 
Roger may have been really deprived of his 
property; but it does not follow that the proofs 
vidueed by him of that fact were perfectly con- | 
vineing, or entirely in accordance with the law. 
And even if they had been such, they might have 
appeared otherwise to the anid judge of the Prize | 
Court; and it is on this account that the Supe- 
rior of Ten rescind the decrees of the Inferior 
‘Tribunals. 

nly, the omission to continue the above- 

cited appeal, can in no way be attributed to the 
judges of this island; neither is it true that any 
‘threats were made use of towards the above- 
mentioned attorney. Such a course would have 
been diametrically opposed to the statutes of my 
Order ; neither would its members have dared to : 
act in such a manner, either against foreigners or , 
the inhabitants my subjects, without incurring a + 
heavy re 

Fiaally” as it is impossible for my knights, 
putting aside the order of right, and neglecting * 
the rule of our statutes, to restore to the above- | 
mentioned Roger that which he claims, nothing | 
remains in our power but to grant him the fuculty | 
of agai uting his right befure the above- 
mentioned Court of Audience os in law so often 
and earnestly offered to the aforenamed attorney. 
Nor certainly can it be presumed, that your Ma- : 
jesty in your clemency and justice can desire any- | 
‘thing farther. To this conclusion I am the more 
drawn from the deci of the advocate of the ! 
Admiralty himself, for he proposea the granting 
of letters of reprisal not for any other reason than | 
that he supposed justice had been denied to the 
said Rogers and that he haa been precluded from 
the remedy of a Court of Appeal. This having 
been an erroneous conclusion, the entire found- : 
ation of the above-mentioned opinion is wholly | 
removed, And it is the more to be hoped that 
this decision will be approved of by your most 
serene Majesty, as my necessary subjection to the

ON THE DESTRUCTION OF NOXUMENTAL BRASSES. 
Any person might naturally be led to suppose, 

on the many costly and learned works 





which, within the last few ycars, have appeared 
on the subject of monumental brasses, that their 
value was now fully appre 
care was taken to ensure their preservation, OF at 
least prevent their wanton destruction. ut, un- 
huppily, such is far from being the case; and 
though rubbings of brasses are to be found in 
every antiquarian society, and n the possession 
y private individuals, the church 
ther parties on whom their pre 
incipally depends, are for the most 
blind to their mportance as histo- 














servation 
part wilful 


pri 
ly 
ical memorials, and with impunity allow them to 

Iu many of our country, 
and I may alco add own burches, are these in- 
teresting records of the deal stowed away as use~ 


be mutilated or stolen, 





less lumber in the v 
modern pew. 
through the medium of your valuable journal, 
some instances which have fallen under his own 
Observation, in the hope that those who read may 
make some exertions to rectify such ucts of dese- 
cration where hey have already occurred, and to 
prevent their future recurrence. 

To begin, then with the most important as re- 

is the loss neurred by the antiquary, thongh 

all show an equal want of good feeling and neg- 
leet of things sucred, I will first offer the substance 
of a few notes taken during a recent excursion to 
Cobham, Kent. ‘The brasses in this church have 
Jong been noted as presenting some of the most 
interesting ear'y examples of this species of mo 
nument, extending from the year 1320 to 1529, 
‘They exemplify almost every variety of costume 
that prevailed during that period, executed with 
the most artistic skill, and accompanied with the 
most clegant accessories in the shape of canopies, 
brackets, and allegorical designs. Imagine, then, 
the feelings of the antiquary who, upon approach- 
ing the chancel where most of these brasses lie, 
finds that it is flooded with water _ The roof has 
Frslually fallen to decay and the Earl of Darn- 
ley, whose property the chance is, has refused to 
repair it. And yet thissame nobleman ean spend 
thousands of potnds in adorning his seat, Coblam 
Hall, the ancient domain of the family, in whose 
commemorn ion most of these brasses are laid 
down Imay also here mention that part of the 
ood-screen Wiich forms the back of the can’ 

yw has been glazed, in order, I suppose, to kee 
Bat the damp of the chancel, while portion on 
the other side has been entirely cut away. This 
is by far the most flagrant case of neglect wh'ch I 
have ever witnessed; but there are several minor 
instances which well demand exposure. At Men- 
dlesham, Suffolk, is a fine large figure of Juhn

Knyvet, Esq., m armour, almost entirely con- 
cealed by a pew passing up the whole length of 
the brass. Now, for a very little expense, the 
slab might be removed and laid down agala in 
the chancel. At Polstead, in the same county, is 
a small brass of a civilian and family, date about 
1490, hidden in the same manner and a figure of 
a priest in the chasuble, lying loose in the vestry. 
Also at Little Waldingfield is'a brass in memory 
of Robert Appleton and wife, 1526, of which the 
male figure 1s covered by a pew. In Upminster 
Church, Essex, were found, not very long since, 
during he progress of some alterations, £0 lowe 
female figures under the flooring of a pew, which 
Teft to be tossall about in he vestry One 
is an el gant figure of a lacy in heraldic mantle 
and horned head-dress, with a dog-at her feet 
date about 450, the other about 1630, At &. 
James's, Colchester, the head of a figure was long 
eft loose, till at last it has been stolen. And, to 
conclude, pews have lately been Luilt over tvo 
brasses at Margate, one of which is an carly exe 
ample of a skeleton. ‘To these instances, which 
have fallen under my own observation, J doubt 
not that every collector can add several others of 
the same description ; but these are sufficient to 
show the wide extent of the evil, and the necet 
sity of correction,

Authors Trustee Society.— Authors, a8 a clast, 
are perhaps the most unfit men in the world to 
make the most of their own property: and ware 
they ever 40 compatont, ft wil often bappen that 
their works do not attain to any great value as 
copyrights till after the poor author ix laid in bia 
grave. It is then, when his family are somotimes 
exposed to severe distress, that mora favourable 
terms might be obtained from publishers; but 
there is no one loft who is enpable of acting for 
the benefit of the widaw or children. 

A Society might be formed to take charge na 
trustees of the property of an author in his workey 
to make en ents with booksellers for the 

ivilege of publishing future editions as they may 

required, and to take care that the Aonoraeinm 
for each edition ba duly paid into the hands of 
the person who is entitled to receive it, 

‘No expense would attend the formation of such 
a Society. Its mectings could be held at scarcely 
any cost. ‘The advertisements, to announce from. 
time to time what works are open for offers from 
printers, booksellers, and pablishers, would amount 
to n very small sum in the course of the yoar — 
I dare say the Editor of “ N. & Q” would insert 
them gratuitously. But, {f necessary, a small per= 
centage on the fees paid would cover all the dine 
bursements of the Society. 1. PK

Dannocks. — Hedging-gloves maile of whit-lea 
ther (untanned leather), and used by workmen 
catting and trimming fences, are called in this 
part of Norfolk dannocks. Can any of your corre- 
mondents say whence the word is derived 


Edingthorpe. 

__ [tlt should rather be Dornecks," says Forby, “which 
isthe proper Flemish name of Tuxrnai, a Frenchified 
tue, long since universally substituted, Two hun- 
tied years ago it was celebrated for its coarse woollen 
munufictures, principally of carpets and hangings 
mentioned in some of our old comedies Probably 
thick gloves were another article of importation. Our 
madera dannocks, indeed, are of thick leather, and 
made at home by our own glovers. Dan. dorneck."]

Imperfect Bible.— A Bible has lately come into 
Ry pomsession in an imperfect state. It is in 
Wack letter, 4to., with the capitals commencing 
the ch in Roman letters. I wish to know 
the date and printer. It begins at ful. 7., at the 
ad of the 6th verse of xvih chapter of Genesis, 
‘eounted that to him for righteousness.” There 
weanumber of engravings representing the in- 
‘Vou. IX.—No. 230. 




















JIL. 


























struments ured in the temple and tabernacle, at 
fol. 36. 38. 40. 62. 160. &c. There is no date, but 
I think it is about 1590 or 1600. 

Ax Iosonanvs on tas Sosszcr. 


‘This imperfect Bible is one of the very numerous 
series of editions of the Genevan or Puritan version, 
commonly called the Breeches Bible. It is not 
but a pot folio, having six leaves to the sl 
ture, Imprinted at London by the Depu 
topher Barker, printer to the Queen's most excellent 
Maiestie, Anno Dom. 1595. Cum privilegio.” Our 
correspondent’s copy wants the title and preface (three 
leaves), six leaves of Genesis, the title to the N. Testa- 
‘ment, and at the end eleven leaves, including the two 
tables. The translation may be identified by the last 
word of 1 Cor. vi, 9, or by 1 Tim. i, 10, ‘There is 
another edition by the same printer, and of similar 
size, in the year 1602 ; but the title to the second part 
has “ conteineth,” instead of “ conteining.”)

Edward Spencer's Marriage. — Can any reader 
supply me with particulars of the marriage of 
Edward Spencer of Rendlesham, co. Suffolk, and 
Grosvenor Square, who lived in the early part of 
the last century, and whose daughters married the 
Duke of Hamilton and Sir James Dashwood ? 


Cuances Baivorr. 
Keppel St., Russell Sq. 


[The following entry is given in Dary’s Suffolk 
Collections (Add. MSS. 19,097,, p. 272.): “ Edward 
Spencer, son of John Spencer, Fsq., ob. 1718. Edward, 
now living at Naunton Hall, isa barrister-at-law. He 
married Anne, the only daughter of William Baker of 
Layham, clerk, by whom he had issue Henry Spencer, 
who died an ‘inf cer, their only 
daughter, and_now living.” extract is copied 
from Hawes's MSS., the date of which, unfortunately, 
is not given. ]

‘Mr. B,, in preference to being considered as the 
founder of a new family, supposed himeclf, or 
wished to be supposed by others, to be descended 
from an old stock of the same name, he kindly

[The Editor has been informed by a person on 
whose accuracy he can rely, that a lady who received a 
letter from Mr, Spence offering certain information 
respecting his family taken from the Cotgreave pe- 
digree, and who imprudently sent money for the same, 
got nothing but the most absurd rubbish in return, 
and having been induced to make inquiries into the 
subject, was fully satisfied that the whole thing was « 
fraud.) 





LICENCES TO CRENELLATE. 


(Vol. ix. p. 220.) 

The subjoined list of names and places will 
supply Mg, Parner with the counties of all the 
pitcet named in his inquiry, except two in whieh 

spect some error. If farther references to 
authorities are desired, they will be given with 
pleasure in reply to a private application, but 
‘would crowd your pages inconveniently. 

1. Cokefield for Melton—Cokefeud for Moulton, 
Suffolk. 

2. Grisnak for Molun— Query this? 

8, Langeton for Newton in Makerfield—L. for 
‘Newton Hall or Castle, the head of the Palatine Ba- 
rony of Newton, in Lancashire. 

4. Esselynton for Esselynton —E. in Northumber- 
Jand. 

5. Trussel for Cubleston— C. in Staffordshire. 

6. De Ia Beche for De la Beche— De la Beche 
Castle, Aldworth, Berks, 

7. The same for Beaumes— Beaumys Castle, Shin- 
field, Berks,

‘There can, I think, be little doubt that Stans- 
ede, in Mr. J. I. Panxsn’s list, is Stanstead 
Hall, near Halstead in Essex. I have never seen 
Stanstead Hall, but about a month since I was in 
company with the late occupant; from whom I 
Tearned, in casual conversation, ‘that it was an 
ancient house, with moat and fortifications. In 
addition to this I may state, that there are monu- 








ments in the old church (St. Andrew) of Halstead 
to some of the Bourchier family. ‘These facts, 
taken together, seem to fix the locality with sufli- 
cient precision, One of the monuments just re- 
ferred! to is a bras, commemorating Sit Bartio- 
lomew Bourchier and bis two wives: which, when

Heyheved, mentioned in Mr. Paneen’s list, ia 
Highhead Caate in Cumberland. In the reign of 
Exlward IT. it was a peel howe (pelum de Hey- 
heved) possessed by Marcle, Earl of Carlisle 
modern times it became the property of a family 
named Richmond, one of whom erected the 
sent house, after by Inigo Jones. But be 
died before it was finished, leaving co-heirs, who 
quarrelled about the partition of the estate, and 
‘actually put a hedge through the centre of the 
House.” Eventually one-half came into the bands 
of Lord Brougham, who is understood to bere 
purchased the other, and will probably restore the 
whole. a

ples on the practice of interment in churches The 
example of the virtuous and primitive confessor, Arc 
bishop Sancroft, who ordered himself to be buried is 
the churchyard of Fresingfield in Suffolk, thinking & 
improper that the house of God should be made te 
repository of sinful man, ought to command the iai- 
tation of less deserving persons: perhaps it bed an i 
Hucnce over the tind ‘of his. sucacamr, “Arcbudap 
Secker, who ordered himself to be buried in the chore 
yard of Lambeth. ‘The Bishops of London in see 
sion, from Bishop Compton to Bishop Hayter, wh 
died in 1762, inclusive, have been buried in Fue 
Churchyard.”

of the See of Canterbury 
.—As no one bas yet volun- 
Mr. Fraser's question, How the 
letter of Pope Boniface ordaining that, however 
Jaman circumstances might be changed, the city of 
Canterbury should ever thereafter be esteemed 
the metropolitan see, can be reconciled with the 
ion of the archiepiscopal see of Westminster, 
—~T may suggest as a solution this maxim : 

“ Nihil tam conveniens est naturali equitati, unum- 
quodque dimolvi eo ligamine quo ligatum est.” 
possible, too, that Pope Pius IX. may have 
dered that a case had arisen for applying this 
principle, — 
jecessitas publica major est quam private.” 
Bat be this as it may (and you will excuse me in 
observing, by the way, that I do not concur in the 
correctness of this hypothetical view if taken by 











Is is 
cont 





his holiness), I hope we shall hear from Mz. , 


Prasen whether the former of the above maxims 
has been effectual to remove his difficulties, which 
as I presume from their insertion in“ N. & Q,." 
are not of a purely theological nature.

“ Three cats,” Se. (Vol. ix p.173,).— Bras

beginning with “Three cata;" and I beg 
to inform lier, that there never was any more than 














shat she mentions. |The object of the singer was, | 


to cause fun by an claborately modulated cadenza, 
on the word coal-dust, and then to call on the com- 








pany to join in chorus. He next continued 
| some significant word, as “ notwi 7 

| after = pause of some bars rest, he went on with 
“Three cate,” as before, ad infinitum, changing the 
| initial word each time. It required some tact 

‘ give it effect; but, if sung bya clever humorist, 
i was sure to keep the room in o roar of laughter. 
| But its day is gone by. Ganaten.

Views in London by Canaletto (Vol. ix., p. 106.). 
—In reply to the Query of your correspondent 
Goxnoxa, I beg to say that Ihave long had the 
pleasure of postessing one of Canaletto’s London 
views, that of the ‘Thames from the Temple 
Gardens, in which the hand that painted gondolas 
and masks may be traced in Thames wherries and 
grave Templars. believe there are others in the 
collections of the Dukes of Buccleugh and Nor- 
thumberland, Epwuxp Purrrs. 


Park Lane, 





During the residence of Antonio Canaletto at 
Venice, he painted a number of pictures, at low 
Prices or Joreph Smith, Esq, the British consul 
ut that gentleman retailed those paintings at an 
enormous profit to English travellers. Cansletto 
finding this out, was induced to visit a countr; 
where his talents were 20 much appreciated. He 
accordingly came to England in the year 1746, 
being then about fifty years of age. He remained 
with us six or seven years (not foo, as stated by 
Walpole), and during that period received great 
encouragement from the English nobility. His 
delineations of London and its environs, especially 
those of Thames scenery (of which he seems to 
have been very fond), are deservedly admired. 
‘Two of these are at Goodwood, and another (P 
Tiament Street, looking towards Charing Cross 
in the Buccleuch Collection. Several London 
paintings were, at the beginning of the present 
century, in the possession of the Hon. Percy 
‘Wyndham. Some others are to be found in the 
royal collections, and in those of many noblemen 
and gentlemen of fortune. 
Epwasp F, Ruxoavrr, 


London Fortifications (Vol. ix., pp. 174. 207.). 
—During the last civil war a fortification was 
erected at the Brill Farm, near old St. Pancras 
Chureb, where, 120 years after, Somers Town was 
built. ‘A view of it is extant, and may be obtained 
for a few shillings. ‘The Brill is also stated to 
have been a Roman station, but, I believe, without 
foundation. G.J.8.

What Day is it at our Antipodes # (Vol. v 


PP. 102. 649.).— After the able way in which this 
subject has been treated by A.E. B., I will ouly 











add an extract from A 
graphy, by Emanuel Bowen, 
- 250. 


System of Geo- 
ndon, 1747, vol. iii 


“One thing more is worth observing concerning 
this place (Macao), namely, that the Port 
‘Sunday here is the Saturday with the Spaniards of the 
Philippine Islands, and so forward through all tbe 
days of the week, although there be scarce any differ- 
ence in the longitude of both places. But the reeson 
is, the Portuguese, in coming to Europe, pass east- 
ward, whereas the Spaniards, coming from America, 
pass westward ; so that between both, they have sailed 
round the globe: in doing which therc is necessarily 
one day lost, as we have taken occasion to show in the 
introduction to this work.”

NOTES ON BOOKS, ETC. 


‘When Dr. Ure tells us that from tho year 1804 
when he conducted the schools of chemistry and m- 
nufactures in the Andersonian Institution, up to tit 
present day, he has been assiduously 
study and improvement of most of the chemical, asd 
‘many of the mechanical, arte ; that during that period 
he has been habitually consulted professionally by 
proprietors of factories, workshops, and mines 
rectify what was amiss in their establishments, and 
supply what was wanting, he shows cleatly how grat 
were his qualifications for the preparation of 4 Dix 
tionary of Arts, Manufataren, and Mine, conten & 
clear exposition of their principles and practice: sod 
S therefore nt wonder that work Gnderiaken wit 
such advantages should have reached what is nov 
hefore us, a “fourth edition, corrected and greatly & 
larged.” "Dr, Ure has, in this edition, turned to good 
account the many novelties of an interesting and wseil 
nature first displayed in the Great Exhibitios, aad 
his two portly volumes may be consulted with 
vantage not only by manufscturers and 
men, but by lawyers, legislators, and, in short, all wb? 
take’ an interest in those achievements of science 1 
which this great country owes its pre-eminence. 

Unnoticed by reviewers, and unaided by favour of 
influence, Mr. Keightley tells us that his Mythology 
Ancient Greece and Ttaly bas reached its third edition. 
Sormuch the better, for it proves that the book Ms 
merits of its own, and those merits have won for it 
place which will keep Mr. Keightley’s name in me 
mory as long as a love for classical literature and tat 
ful learning remains; and this, we suspect, will be 
longer than Mr. Keightley anticipates. As the suse 
which has attended this valuable and original expastoa 
of classical mythology renders it unnecessary to SY 
one word as to its, we may content ourslte 
with stating that this edition has been carefully 
vised, has received numerous additions, and, altbougt 
it is beautifully got up, is published at a lower prise 
than its predecessor.

BATTLE OF TRAFALGAR AND DEATIC OF NELSON. 


‘The following a qe letter, as a historical 
doeament, is worth preserving in the pages of 
“N.& Q" Itrelates to the im i national 
events of the battle of Trafalgar and dexth of 
Nelon. Tho writer way at the time a. slgnal 
midshipman in the service, and eg 

teen yours of age. He was a native of Glasgow, 
and dled many years since, much respected, 


H.M.8, Defence, 
At anchor off Cadiz, 28 Oct, 1805. 
My dear Betty [the writer's sister], 

T have now the pleasure of writing you, after a 
noble victory over the French aad Spanish fleets, 
on the 21st Octol Cape We have 
token, burnt and sunk, gone on 
one sail of the line, ‘The names 
know after, On the 19[th} our frigates mi 
signal ; the Combined Fleets were coming out; 10 
as we were stationed between the frigate and our 
fleet, we repented ditto to Lord Nelson. It being 
calm we could not anake much way, but in the 
courss of the night we got astrong broces, and 
hext morning our frigate made the signal for 
them, being all at sea. So on the afternoon of the 
20[th] we saw them to leeward; but it was blow- 
ing fresh and very hazy, #0 Lord Nelson 
our signal for a captain; #0 our cuptain went om 
board, and Lord Nelson told Ceptain Hope he

doctrine was settled shortly after the 
Conqus ih in oti of law. 

U Quia 18 Edw. La 
tenant in fee simple might grant lands to be holden. 
by the grantee and his heirs of the grantor aud is 
heirs, subject to feudal services and to escheat ; 
and by such subinfeudation manora were cre 

‘The above-named statute forbade thefuture sube 
infeudation of lands, and consequently bin 
the further creation of manors. ie the stabute 
@ sclice of the fee can but transfer bis teaure. 
‘There are instances ia which one manor is holden 
of another, both Laving been created before the 
statute, 

In the instance mentioned by J. B. it is pre- 
sumed that tho hamlet escheated to the heirs of 
A. on failure of the heirs of B. (See the statute 
De Donis Conditionatibus, 13 Baw. 1.) 

Tt is not, and never was, necessary, or even 
possible, that the Jord of a manor should be the 
owner of all the lands therein; on tho contrary, 
if he w ere would be no manor; for a manor 
cannot subsist without a court baron, nnd thers 
can be no auch court unless thers are 
tenants (at least two in number) holding of the 
lord, The land retained by the lord consists of 
his own demesne and the wostes, which last ‘com= 
prise the highways and commons. If tho lord 
should alienate all the lands, but retain hie lord~ 
ship, the latter becomes a reignory in grove. 

uch was and is the tenure of lands in England,

MARMONTIXTO, om SAND-PATNTING. 
(Vol. ix, p.217.) 


Mr. Hoes, a native of Bibrach, in Germany, 
was accustomed to lay claim to the invention of 
sand-painting ; and would often with a little pride 
ropeat to his friends the way in which it wus first 
suggosted to his mind. Simply this: —Once, while 
ho was engaged ornament ¥ ‘ plateau with an 
elaborate and rich design, King George Lil. en- 
tered the apartment; and after having regarded 
the design and modam operandi {yr some com- 
siderable time in silence, exclaimed, in an impa- 
tient manner, as if vexed that 20 much beauty 
should be eo short-lived: “Haas! Haas! you 
ought to fasten it,” Brom that moment, the artist 
turned his ingenuity to the wabject: and how 
successfully, his pictures show. 

The remarks of F.C. H. as to the mode of 
painting are quite correct ‘The Gxing, of Sum 















sanil was the last operation, inasmuch as Dave 
hoard of tho artist's wrath visiting a poor pury 
Decause she had shaken # picture, snd 
Giurbed the sand not yet cal The secre died 
ith bim and a friend, a contemporancous artist, to 
whom I believe he had communicated 
this friend's name T do not. know. 





‘had o eye London for some time (I believe 
in Regent Street), where there were portraits 
done in sand. A portrait of himself was con- 
‘sidered the gem of the pictures: such a vitality 
and delicacy of colouring did it possess, I mcn- 
tion this merely to show that sant coul ap> 
ie to other tranches of art besides a 

we history of the pictures ut Windsor Castle is 
to be seen in one of the oll Windsor Guides, 
Mr. Haas died at Bibrach, where doubtless many 
of his pictures are, 

“paintings cannot last long; they have in 
themselves the element of their own destruction, 
“their rough surface,” which very soon collects 
and retains the dust, [never heard of their being 
cleaned. Jour Mumauer.

Sc. “Tt is mentioned in the Calendar of Patent

In our previous list an error occurred, which 
we would wish to correct. The last letter of 
Henry VILL was addressed to the Grand Master 
Pierre Du Pont, and not to Nicholas Cotoner, who 


ascended the Maltese throne in 1663. The trans~ 
Jation of H. M.'s congratulatory letter to Du Poot, 
‘on his election, we trust you bave rer 
ceived. We referred in our former Note to a 
letter of Charles IL, under date of “the last day 
of November, 1674,” and since that came to our 
observation we have seen an excl copy bearing 
the autograph of the king. ‘This circumstance 
Tends us to inquire at what period, and with what 
English monarch, the custom of sending dupli- 
cate letters originated? In the time of James 

it would appear to have been followed, as one of 
H. M's letters is thus marked in his own hand 
writing. 

‘We would state, before closing this Note, that 
the letters of Jumes IL, are the earliest in date of 
any English royal levters filed away at thie island 
which ure counteraig or bear the address of the 
Grand Master at the foot of the first on the 
left-hand side, as is customary ia writing official 
letters to government at the present times 

Will any of your correspondents kindly inforsa 
us with what English monarch the custom ori=

“Verbatim et literatim."—As this phrase often 
finds insertion, even in the pages of “ N. & Q," it 
may be well to call attention to the fact, ‘that there 
is no such adverb as Jiteratim in the Latin lan- 
guage. There Is the adverb Literate, which means 
after the manner of a literate man, learnedly ; but 
to express the iden Intended by the coined word 
Yiteratin, T think we must use the form ad literam— 
L, HL J. Towwa. 





“ Verbatim et ad litera.”

‘keray's; written, we are told, *one morning last 
in tho Messenger office,’ dnring a call made by 
author: — 
+ Werthier had a Tove for Charlotte, 
Such ax words gould never utter. 
Would you know how fiest ho met her? 
‘She was cutting bread and butter. 
* Charlotte was a married Indy, 
ada man wat oF 5 
And for all the wealth of Indies, 
‘Would do nothing that might hurt her, 
+80 be sigh’d and pined, and ogted, 
‘And his passion boil'd and bubbled, 
‘Till he blew his silly braine out, 
‘And no nore by them was troubled, 
* Charlotte, having seen bis body 
rue before er on a shutter, 
Like a well-conducted perron, 
‘Went on cutting bread and butter.”

* Vanitatem observare" (Vol.ixy pp.247.$911.)— 
‘The quotation of R. H.G. is no more to be found 
in the Canons of Laodicea than in those of An= 
cyra, Indeed the passage has more the 
‘ance of a recommendation, certainly excellent, 
than of any grave decree of a council, It can 
Lardly be supposed to. bear any other meaning 
than that Christian females ought not to indulge 
vty, or take occasion to be vain of thelr works 
wool, spun or woven; but to refer all their 
talent to the Almighty, who gives to them the 
skill and ability to work, Here is evidently an 
allusion to the skill and wisdom given to Bescleel 
and Ooliab= 

« Both of thern hath he instructed with wisdom, to 
do .... tapestry and embroidery in blue and purple,

ME, JUSTICE TALYOURD.

those thoughts which filled his mind 


Wery eve of quitting the high aud honourable
