‘After speaking of the poculiar ‘aspect of erimo 
in that part of the country where be dulivered bis 
Tast ebarge, he goes oa to say : 


* T cannot help mysolf thinking it may be in no emall 
dogree attributable fo that separation between class und 
claw, which ie the great curve of British society, and 
for which we are all, more of les, in our respective 
spheres, in some degree responsible, and whieh is more 
complete in those districts than in agricultural districts, 
where the resident gentry are enabled to abed around 
them the blessings resulting from Ue excreise of bene: 
rolenes, and the influence and exaraple of uctive kind- 
nose, Lam afraid we all of ux keep too much aloof 
from thors beneath us, and whom we thus encourage 
to look upon us with suspicion and dislike. Even to 
our servants we thinks perhaps we fuldl our duty when 
‘wo perform our contract with them—when we pay them 
their wages, and treat then with the civility consintent 
with our babiteand fel /hen we eur our temper, 
and uve no violent exprowions towards them. But 
how painful is the thought, that there are men and 
women growing up around us, ministering to our com~ 
forts and necessities, conti ly inmates of our dwell- 
ings, with whove affections and nature we ave as much 
‘unacquainted ax if they were the inhabitants of some 
other sphere, ‘This feeling, arising from that kind of 
reserve peculiar to the English character, does, 1 think, 
greaily tend to prevent that mingling of class with class, 
that reciprueation of kind words and gentle affections, 
gracious admonitions and kind inquiries, which often, 
more than any book-elucation, tend to the culture of 
the affections of the heart, refinement and elevation of 
the cliracter of those to whom they are addressed. 
And if T were to be asked what ix the great want of 

‘nglish society—to mingle elass with elnss—L would 
tay, in one word, the want in the want of sympathy.”

ty the Pralms in the Syriac Version. — 
T, J. Bocnrow (Vol. ix, p. 242.) obsorves, 
to the superseripti 2 
the chief performer that 
Syriac and Arabic versions omit this super= 
ion altogether, from ignorance of the musical 
ofthe words.” And lower down he speaks 
if ipa wore expressed in the Syrine by the 
eburch.” To not question the accuracy 
Ma B's renderings of the Hebrew words, for 



















they have been admitted for centuries ; but Lwisk: 
to observe that the translator of the Syriac should. 
not be lightly charged with ignorance of Hebrew, 
as I oan testify from an extensive acqusintance 
with that venerable version. I therefore cannot 
allow that the words were omitted by the trans= 
lator for that reason, Besides, whenever he found 
a word untranslateable, be transferred it as it 
was. Nor do I admit that nehiloth, in Paalea vu is 
translated by the term “church.” And this leads 
me to remark, what scems to have been over« 
looked by most writers, viz» that the Syriso ver 
sion omits uniformly the titles of the Penlms as 
they are found in Hebrew.* Tho inscriptions 
contained in the common editions of these Palms 
form no part of the translation. One of them 
refers to the destruction of Jerusalem by Titus! 
They are not always the sume. Iam ncequainted 
with at least three diforent sats of these headings 
contained in the Syriac MSS. in the British Mu 
soum. Erpeniua omitted thom altogether in his 
edition of the Paalter, and Dathe's follows hi © 
which very substantial reusona are given by him 
in tho “ Proof. ad Leet." of his yrium Syria- 
cun, pp- 36, 97. Halas, 1768, B. 


“ Old Rowley" (Vol.ix. p.235.).—The nick 
name of * Old Rowley,” as applied to Charles IL, 
seems to be derived from Roland, and has refer+ 
ence to the proverbial saying, “A Roland for an 
liver ;" the former name being given to Charles 
in contradistinction to the Protectors name of 
Oliver. Roland and Oliver were two celebrated. 
horses, or, as some say, two pages of Charlemagne 
possessing equal qualitica: and hence, “I'll give 
you a Roland for your Oliver” was tantamount 
to “I'll give you as good as you send."F N. LJ.

‘TNE LHOEND OF THE SEVEN SE9TERS, 


Will the Editor of *N.& Q,," or any of his cor- 
respondents, kindly inform me of the true eirwum= 
stancesfrom which the following legend has: £ 
The locality which wan the scene of the tragedy is 
the little villoge of Ballybunion, situnted withiu a 

of Kerry Head. ‘The scenery round 

dest and most striking description. 

Browsing, lids, rising abruptly out of the 
water to the height of over one hundred feet, and 
numerous caves, into which the 


‘one of those prrecipices we havo mentioned, thero 
stands tho zaina of a cutie, soamingly of great 
‘antiquity. Nothing now remins but thi basement 
storey, and that esems as if it would be able to 
withstand the war of winds and waves for hundreds 
of years longer, According to the legend, this 
cnatle was inhabited by a gallant chieftain at the 
period of the incursions of the anes, and who wes 
father of seven blooming daughters. He wes 
himself a brave warrior, animated with the greatest 
hatrod against the Ostmen, who, at that period, 
were laying every part of Erin woste, His sword 
yer roster! in its sheath, and day nnd night hi 
ight gallies cruised about the const on the watch 


ro painful denth, Conveyed to the forteesr, 

ounds were drewed, and they were allowed 

the fr F the castle, Here, gradually a 

love sprang between them and the seven Irish 
maidens, who yielded to thelr ardent provestati 

ud agrood to ly with them t Denmark. Every 

ig was arranged for the voyuge, and one fear 

iglt in winter was chosen for the 

attempt. Not asingle star shone in the sky, the 
1d blast care sweeping from the ocean, the 

fell in torrents, ani the water roared umd raged 

with terrific violence ail the rocky caverns. 

Escaping down from the battlement by a rope 

ladder, they discovered to their horror, that on 

e gvound they were surronaied Sry 


armed men. Not a word wes were wan Saey

well knew into whose hands they had fallen. Con- 
ducted again within the fortress, they found them- 
selves fuce to face with their i ther, One

man, on’ receiving the command, recoiled a few 
paces, transfixed with horror; and then he advanced 
nearer, and seemed as if remonstrating with him. 
Bat the parent's face sarumed an abiolutely de~ 
it sion ; and more peremptorily repeat- 
he stalked out of the room. Aad 
now commenced a fearful scene. ‘I'he lovers were 
torn from each other's 
Drought forth ag 
violent, and the spray ing 
ff, whilst the vivid flashes of lightning afforded 
‘ horrible illumination to the dreary scene. Pro- 
ceeding along the brink of the precipice, they at 
length came to a chasm which resembled somewhat 
‘the crater of a volcano, as it was completely closed, 
with the exception of the opening at the top, and 
cone small aperture below, through which the sca 
ed with terrible violence. ‘The rolling of the 
aters sounded fearfully on the ear of those around, 
and now at length the sisters divined their fate. 
One by one they were hurled into the boiling flood: 
one wild shriek, the billows closed again, and all 
was over. What the fate of their lovers was, the 
legend says not. ‘The old castle hes crumbled into 
Tuins—t feain sleeps in an unknown grave, 
is very name forgotten ; but still the sad ending 
of the imaidens is remembered, and even unto this 
day the cavern is denominated the “Cave of the 
Seven Sisters.” Such is the above legend as it 
still exists amongst the peasantry, and any of your 
contributors would extremely oblige by informing 

me of the name of the Iriah leader.

of the public prayers, with which 
Fesuues of tosh doy, artued sha alndlont at tha 
youngest and most thoughtlewe; th excellence of hie 
moral characters his gravity blended with cheerfisl= 
ness, his strletness joined with gontleness, his favour to 
the virtuous and diligent, and even the mildnew of his 
reproofs to thove who were Jess attentive, reodered biz 
the object of their respect and admiration. Never was 
more exact discipline presorved than in his class, nor 
evor anywhere by more gentle means, His sway was 
absolute, because it wax founded in reawn and coffe 
tion, He nover employed a hare epithet in finding 
fault with any of his pupils; and when, Instead of 
they deserved, they. 
met merely with a mild reproof, It was conveyed in 
such @ manner as to throw pat only the delinquent, 
but sometimes the whole class into tears To gain bis 
favour was the highest ambition of every student; and 
the gentlest word of disapprobation was a punishment, 
to avoid which, no exertion wax deemed too much. 
His great object was not merely to make bis pupils 
philosophers, but to render them anew plows 
Christians, Toyal to thelr king, and attached to the 











Beitish constitution; pure in morals, happy in tho 
comeiousnes of a right conduct, and frieads to all 
mankind,”

Aoscribed as rang oa Shrove at Newbury, 
was no doubt the old summons which used to call 
‘our ancestors to the priest to be shrived, or con 
fessed, on that day. It is commonly called the 
“Pancake Bell,” because it was also the signal 
for the cook t0'put the on the fire. This 
savoury couplet occurs im Poor Rodin for 1684: 

“ But hark, I hear the pancake bell, 

And fritters make a gallant sell." 


Tho custom of ringing this bell bas been retained 
det many parishes, It is orthodosly tung, wt Bo

Theodore de la Guard. — 1 have a tract by him 
with the title of The simple Cobler of Ai hy 
ia Fregeteg London, 1647, Who was ho? and 
where can 1 find any account of him or his ree ? 

se 

[The Rev. Nathaniel Ward was the author of this 
work. He was born at Haverhill in Essex, of whlch 
place hix father was « clergyman ; and after studying 
at idge, booame minister of Standon in Hexte; 
dul was cited Defore the bishop, Dee, 19. 163, t0 
anewer for bit nonconformity. Being forbidden to 
preaoh, he embarked for America in April, 1634, and 
Settled as pastor of the church at Ipswich, or Aggswam, 
He returned to England in 16%¢, and on June 90, 
1647, preached before the House of Commons, and 
the same year published The Simple Cotler, To wax 
afterwards settled at Shootield, nosr Brontwood, where 
he died in 1653, in his vighty-third year. Fuller, in 
his Worthies, e0. Suffolk, speaking of liim, says, that he, 
“following the counsel of the poet, 

+ Ridentom dicers verum, 

Quis votes?” 

“What doth forbid that one may snl 
And also tell the truth the while ? 
hath in a gesting way, in some of hix books, delivered 
much smart truth of the present timex” Dr. Mather, 
in his Mignatia, remarks of him, tlat “he was the 
author of many composure full of wit and sense 5 
among whieh that enticled Zhe Simple Cobler (which 
most 

















demwoustrated hin 0 be a subtil statesman) was 
considered.” Th yawsed through several edie 
tions in England Tt was reprinted in Boston 
in 1713. The best edition, containing the author's 
subsequent additions, is that edited by David Pulsifer, 
Boston, 1849, ]

BAREELL'S REGIMENT. 
(Vol.ix., pp. 69, 159.) 


Your correspondent H. B. C. is undoubtedly 
correct in his statement that “Ton times a day 
whip the Barrels,” 1s a rogimental parody on tho 
ne, * Methat has the best Wife,” sung in Charles 
fey's musical farce of The Devil 10 Pay, pub 
in 3731. Popular songs have been’ made 
the subject of political or personal parodies from 
time immemorial ; and no more fruitful locality 
for parodies can be found than a barrack, where 
the individual traits of character are so fully de« 
veloped, and afford o full a scope to the talents of 
‘a satirist. Indecd, I knew an officer, who 
recently retired from the service, who seized on 
ular ballad, and parodied it, in cone 
Pexion with regimental affairs, vo the delight of 
his brother officers; and in many instances bis 
parodies wero far moro witty than the original 
‘comnle songs whence they were taken. 

‘As regards the regiment known aé Barrell's, at 
the period assigned as the date of the song relative 
ph oa ‘Le. ciroa 1747, there can be no 
doubt as to what corp: luded to, Burrell's 

riment, now the 4th, or King's Own, regiment, 

infantry, is the only corps that was ever known 
io the British army as Barrel's; for although 
‘Colonel William Barrel! was colonel of the present 




















28th regimant from Sept. 27, 1715, to As 25, 
1730, and of the present 23nd eee the 
latter date to August , 1734, yet neither of those 
regiments appears to have seen any war-sorvice 
during tho periods that they were by 
we boen known in milit ii as 
iments, Ho was appointod to the 4th 
regiment of infantry August 8, 1734, and retained 
tho command of that distinguished exact 
fifteen yoars, for ho died August 9, 1749. Whi 
he. com: the regiment it ‘embarked for 
Flanders, and soryed the campaign of 1744, under 
Field-Marshal Wade, It remained in Flanders

regiment. In 1739 he was farther promoted to

necessarily remove him from’ the personal com- 
mand of lis regiment, He was not t at the

__ Miss Elstob (Vol. iii, p.497):—This surname 
isso uncommon that Thave met with but thres 
instances of persons bearing it; one was the lady 
referred to by your correspondent, the second was 
her brother, Rev. William Elstob, aud the 
third was Dryden Elstob, who served some 
time in the Srd Light Dragoons, and also, I be« 
lieve, in the Royal Navy, —at least I know that he 
used to wear a naval uniform in the streets of 
London. I believe that the family was settled at 
one time nb Newcastle-on-Tyne.* What is known 
of the family ? Juyenss.

HERBERT'S “CHURCH PORCH.” 
(Vol. ix., p. 173.) 

Lventure the followin, 
curious stanza in George Herbert's Church Porch, 
referred to by your correspondent 8. SINGLETON : 

“ God made me one man; love makes me no more, 

‘Till labor come and make my weakness score.” 

It you are single, give all you have to the ser- 
vice of God. But do not be anxious to make the 
gift larger by toil: for God only requires that 
which is suitable to the position in which He has 


placed you. He bestows a certain “estate” upon | 


every man as He bestows life: let both be dedi- 
cated to Him, For if you give first yourself, and 
then what He has given you, this is sufficient; 
ou need not try to be more rich, that you may 
Re more charitable. But if you chooea a life of 
Iabour to gain an “estate” beyond the original 
position assigned to you in the providence of God, 
then you must reckon yourself responsible for the 
“one man” which God “made” you, and for the 
other which you make yourself besides. 

Tconceive the stanza to be a recommendation 
of the contemplative life with poverty, in pre- 
ference to the active life with ri J. HB. 


ANCIENT USAGES OF THE CHURCH, 
(Vol. ix., pp. 127. 257.) 


As your well-known correspondent from Clyst 
St. George has addressed an inquiry to you on 
this subject, it may not be uninteresting to some 
of your readers to Jearn that the practice of kneel- 
ing at funcrals still exists in this neighbourhood. 
On a cold December day have I seen men, women, 
and children bend the knee on the bare sod, 
during the Lord's and the other prayers used in the 
outdoor portion of our service, not rising till the 
valedictory grace concluded the service." Indeed, 
I have never known (at least the majority of) 
those attending our funerals here, omit this old 
custom. 

‘That of dressing graves with flowers, at Easter 
and Whitsuntide, prevails here as in Wales: and 
the older folks still maintain the ancient practice 
of an obeisance as often as the Gloria occurs 
during the ordinary services. ‘The last railful of 
communicants are also in the habit of remaining 
in their place at the altar rails till the service is 
concluded; but whether these observances are 
widely spread, or merely local, I have not bad 
sufficient opportunity to judge. J.T. P. 

Dewchurch Vicarage. 

At the church of South Stoke, near Arundel, I 
have lieard the clerk respon after the Gospel: 
“Thanks be to God for the Holy Gospel” 





as the meaning of the | 


|! At Southwick, near Brighton, the rector was 
j wont (about four years since) to stand up at the 
“Glory” in the Litany. 
The Bishop of tomioa believes bowing the head 
| when the doxology, or ascription of praise, is pre- 
nounced, to be a novelty in our Church (Letter 
to the Knightsbridge Churchwarden, March 38, 
| 1854). I remember an old woman ly a 
tending the services of Exeter Cathedral, who was 
wont always to curtsy at the “Glory.” And in 
The Guardian of April 25, W. G. T. alludes to 
2 parish in Staffordshire where the custom prevails 
And A. W. says: 
| <n the western counties of England there are may 
parishes where the custom of bowing at the‘ Glora’ 
has been universally observed by the poor from time 
immemorial. I could mention parishes in Worceste~ 
shire or Herefordshire where it has always prevailed.” 


It should be observed, that the custom is not 
bow at the “Glory” only, but whenever, in te 
course of the service, the names of the Three Pe 
sons of the Blessed "Trinity are mentioned. Se 
Isaiah, vi. 2, 3. 

Thave heard sermons commenced in the name 
of the Holy Trinity, and ended with “ the Glory” 
the preacher repeating the former part and the 
congregation the latter. I believe this is agree- 
able to very ancient use. Can any one say whe 
ther it has anywhere been retained in our own 
Church ? J. W. Hawsrt. 


The custom of Lincolnshire mentioned by Me 
Extacomne as observed by his two parisbioners st 
Bitton had its origin doubtless in the first rubrie 
to the Order for the Administration of the Lords 
Supper in our Book of Common Prayer, which 
enjoins that — 

“So many as intend to be partakers of the Holy 
Communion, shall signify their names to the Curate at 
Jeast some time the day before.” 
| On this Bishop Wilson remarks : 


“It is with great reason that the Chureh has given 
this order; wherefore do not neglect it.” 


“You will have the comfort of knowing, cither that 
your Pastor hath nothing to say against you, or, if be 
ill have the benefit of his advice : ands 

attend your obedience to the 








Church's orders.” 
Gsonor E, Faert 


Reverence to the Altar (Vol. vi.,p.1 
XI. Such obeisance was always made in the eal 
lege to which I belonged, at Oxford, ta the Pro 
vost by every scholar, and by the Bible clerks 
when they procecded’ from their seats to the 
eagle lectern, to read the lessons of the day.

“ Go to Bath” (Vol, ix. p.421.).— T have little 
doubt but that this phrase is connected with the 
fact of Bath's being proverbially the resort of 
beggars; and what more natural, to one ac- 
quainted with this fret. than to bid an impartue 
nate applicant betake himself thither 10 join his 
fellows? Seo also Fuller's Worthies (co. Somere 


set).

who have not the book at hand:

natives there, otters ‘thither from all parte of 
1d; the poor for alms, the pained for ease, 
should fowl flock in a hard frost, but to the 

rn-door? Here, all the two sewsons, being the 
geveral conflvenor of gentry, Indeed lawn are daily

be the proper plaister for the cramp of laziness, yet 
some pity is due to impotent persons, In a word,

R. R. inquires the origin of the above saying, 
but bas forgotten the context, viz, « and get your 
head shaved.” I have often beard it explained as 
an allusion to the fact, that, in former days, per- 
‘sons who showed symptoms of insanity were sent 
to Bath to drink the medicinal waters ; the pro- 
cets of shaving the head being previously resorted 
to. ‘The saying is applied to those who either 
relate “crack-brained” stories, or propose under- 
taking teat edna x orl en table san * 


Ridings and Chaj (Vol. ix, p. $70.).— 
Though unable to give Mz. Tuomas Russauy 
Porree any information respecting the “Ridings 
and Chaffings” of Nottinghamshire and Leicester- 
shire, I send the following note of a somewhat 
similar custom prevalent in Oxfordshire (I never 
heard of it elsewhere), thinking it may perhaps 
interest him and others of your correspondents. 

Iremember once, about three years ago, I was 
walking in Blenbeim Park, with a friend then 
resident at Woodstock, when suddenly the still- 
ness of a summer evening was broken by stran; 
and inharmonious sounds, coming to us across the 
water from the old town. The sounds grew louder 
and louder, and in great surprise I appealed to my 
friend for an explanation; when I earned. that it 
was a custom in that part of the country, when- 
ever it was discovered that a man had been 
beating his wife, for the neighbours to provide 
themselves with all sorts of instruments, fire-irons, 
kettles, and pots, in fine, anything capable of 
making a noise, and proceed en masse to the house 
of the offender, before whose door they performed 
in concert, till their indignation subsided or their 
arms grew weary; and that the noise we then 
heard was the distant sound of such music. 

Ido not know if my friend gave any name to 
this Practices if he did, I have since forgotten it. 
Doubtless, some of your Oxford readers can assist 
me. RB. V.T. 





Mincing Lane, 


At Marchington, in Staffordshire, the custom | 
exists of having what is called a Rantipole Rid- | 
ing” for every man who beats his wife. The , 
ceremony is performed with great care and so- | 
lemnity. A committee is formed to examine into ' 
the case, Then the village poet is employed to | 
give a history of the occurrence in verse. The 
Procession goes round in the evening with a cart, 
which serves as a stage on which the scene is acted 


and from which the verses are recited. ‘The cus- \ 


tom has been there observed, with #o much judg: 
ment and discretion, that it has been productive 
of much good, and has now almost entirely pat s 
atop to this disgraceful practice. I can remember 
several “ridings” in my younger days. HB 





‘Mu. Porrse will find, upon referring to Voli 
Pp. 245., that this custom prevails in Choucester 
shire, with the substitution of straw for chaff. 1 
have seen the Gloucestershire version both ia 
Kent and Sussex, and have received an exple 
nation of it similar to Mz. Porrer’s own suppo- 
sition. G. Wiri1am Sxrane.

“ Branks” (Vol. ix. p. 336.) 
does occur in Burns, and signi 
but it is not in that sense it is used by Wodrow. 
The branks of the Covenanters was an iron collar 
and chain firmly fixed to a tree, or post, or pills, 
about three feet from the ground, ae ee 
locked round the neck of the luckless offender, 
who was thus obliged to remain in » most itr 
convenient and painful crouching posture, bese 
neither able to stand nor lie. Many of these st 
still to be seen in the neighbourhood of the ret 
dences of old Highland families who, ere 
Hardwicke's Jurisdiction Act, exercised the por! 
of pit and gullows. ‘There is one at the entrant 
to Culloden House, near Inverness.

© That no sculptor anxious to advance his own re-

BE old ie may acknowledge its merity and

the will not look up to its designer as his master and

Again, the efforts of genius are cramped by 
eaeoapron ing terms “The feet must un- 
avoidably irocted towards the east; still, 
Sehatever | he situation of the tomb may chance to

whether the light may fall on his side 2 on that, 
no way of escape is open, and no ingenul 
to grapple with the uncontrollable ob 
struction, Portrait painters can choose the posi- 
Gou most favourable to the features, but the mo- 
ae pape of the nineteenth century may

3 the contour of the fies, ani Ps wie 
expanse of brow, which might pro! ve the 
an lively indications of watt taal pas anal 
ability of disposition, and devout tranquillity of 
raust be sacrificed to this unbending law 
ich altereth not." Soulptors, we ure told, 
should overcome difficulties; but here they are 
Fequired to “strive with impomibilities, yeu get 
the better of them." Whether painted windows, 
‘8r some other ornament, or a tomb alone in bar- 
=a h the architecture (the form and features 
Of the individual being elsewhere el), may 
constitute » more desirable memorial, is & 20) 
quostion, but as statues are only admissible in a 
Fecumbent posture, some little latitude aust be 
wed. Like our reformers | in igher things, it 
us to discard what is objectionable in at, 
while we cherish that vii is to be lived. 
Instead of treading in the footsteps of those lofty 
wpirits, we should endeavour to follow the same 
road. Fully appreciating their excellences, fet 
ws avoid the distorted drawit 
their irregulacities in architectural design,
